steps.title: Identify the punctuation problem at the blank
steps.content: The blank sits between two independent clauses: “Jamaican British artist Willard Wigan is known for his remarkable microsculptures” and “Wigan’s sculptures are made from tiny natural materials, such as spiderweb strands” (with the noun phrase “creations so small they can only be seen through a microscope” modifying the subject of the second clause). With option B — ‘microsculptures, creations’ — a comma alone joins two independent clauses, creating a comma splice. A comma cannot properly join independent clauses.

steps.title: Use a period to establish sentence boundaries
steps.content:
A period fixes a comma splice by establishing a proper sentence boundary.

Option C uses a period after “microsculptures,” separating the two independent clauses into complete sentences.

Options A, B, and D fail to resolve this — A is a run-on (fused sentences with no punctuation between the clauses), while B and D leave the comma splice intact.

This matches choice C — C is correct.
